title: Theory of flight
subtitle: Short modules with a quiz at the end. Pass the quiz, then try it in the simulator.

quiz.title: Quiz
quiz.wrong: Not quite — the right answer is highlighted.
quiz.passed: Module passed — you are ready for the simulator!
quiz.failed: Review the lessons and try again.

principles-of-flight.title: Principles of flight
principles-of-flight.description: The four forces, the three axes of control, and why airplanes stay in the air.

four-forces.title: The four forces
four-forces.body: Four forces act on every aircraft: lift pushes it up, weight pulls it down, thrust moves it forward, and drag holds it back. Lift is created by the wings as air flows over them — the faster you fly, the more lift you generate. Straight and level flight is simply these four forces in balance.

three-axes.title: The three axes of control
three-axes.body: An aircraft rotates around three axes. Pitch (elevator) raises or lowers the nose, roll (ailerons) banks the wings, and yaw (rudder) points the nose left or right. To turn, pilots bank the wings and let the tilted lift pull the aircraft around the turn.

speed-and-stall.title: Throttle, airspeed and stall
speed-and-stall.body: The throttle controls engine power, and power controls your airspeed. Wings need a minimum airspeed to produce enough lift: fly slower than that and the wing stalls — it stops lifting and the nose drops. To recover from a stall, lower the nose, apply full throttle and let the speed build up again.

q1.question: Which force keeps an aircraft in the air?

q2.question: Banking the wings with the ailerons is called…

q3.question: What happens if airspeed drops below stall speed?
options: The wing stops producing enough lift

q4.question: How do you recover from a stall?
options: Lower the nose and apply full throttle
Pull the nose up as hard as you can
Cut the throttle and hold the stick still

q5.question: Which force slows an aircraft down?

q6.question: Which force does the engine produce?

q7.question: Straight and level flight means…
options: The four forces are in balance
Only lift and weight are acting
The engine is at idle power

q8.question: Which control surface raises or lowers the nose?

q9.question: How does a pilot turn the aircraft?
options: By banking the wings with the ailerons
By pulling the nose straight up

q10.question: What happens to lift as you fly faster?

cockpit-instruments.title: Cockpit instruments
cockpit-instruments.description: Altimeter, airspeed indicator and attitude indicator — reading the panel at a glance.

altimeter.title: The altimeter
altimeter.body: The altimeter shows your height. It works by measuring air pressure: the air gets thinner as you climb, and the instrument converts that pressure drop into an altitude reading. Always check your altitude before manoeuvring — close to the ground there is no room to correct mistakes.

speed-horizon.title: Airspeed and the artificial horizon
speed-horizon.body: The airspeed indicator measures how fast the air flows over your wings — the speed that keeps you flying, so it is the instrument to watch to stay away from a stall. The attitude indicator shows an artificial horizon: whether the nose is above or below the horizon and how much you are banked, even when you cannot see outside.

compass-heading.title: Compass and heading
compass-heading.body: The compass shows the direction the nose is pointing, measured in degrees: 0° is north, 90° east, 180° south and 270° west. Pilots navigate with headings, not street names: 'turn to heading 270' simply means turn until the nose points west. You have the same heading readout in the simulator HUD.

q2.question: Which instrument shows your bank and pitch relative to the horizon?

q4.question: Which instrument do you watch to stay clear of a stall?

q6.question: How does the altimeter measure your altitude?

q7.question: Which instrument keeps you oriented inside a cloud?

q9.question: Why watch your altitude closely near the ground?
options: There is no room to correct mistakes
The altimeter is less accurate there

q10.question: What does the airspeed indicator measure?
options: How fast air flows over the wings
How fast the aircraft moves over the ground

weather-basics.title: Weather for pilots
weather-basics.description: Wind, clouds and visibility — and the weather every pilot avoids.

wind.title: Wind: friend and enemy
wind.body: Aircraft take off and land facing the wind. A headwind shortens the take-off run and lets you touch down slower and softer; a tailwind does the opposite and can make a runway too short. A crosswind blows you sideways off the runway line, so pilots correct with rudder and bank until touchdown.

clouds-visibility.title: Clouds and visibility
clouds-visibility.body: Visual flying depends on seeing the horizon and the terrain. Low clouds and fog can hide both within seconds, so visual pilots keep a safe distance from clouds and never fly into them: inside a cloud, without instrument training, you quickly lose all sense of up and down.

dangerous-weather.title: Weather to avoid
dangerous-weather.body: Thunderstorms are the most dangerous weather: they hide violent updrafts and downdrafts, hail and ice that can overwhelm any small aircraft. Icing is treacherous too — ice on the wings changes their shape and destroys lift. The rule is simple: check the forecast before flying, and if the weather looks doubtful, stay on the ground.

q1.question: Why do aircraft take off and land facing the wind?
options: The ground run is shorter and touchdown is slower
The view is better for the pilot
It saves fuel on the way out

q2.question: Which wind pushes you sideways off the runway line?

q3.question: What makes thunderstorms so dangerous?
options: Violent updrafts and downdrafts, hail and ice

q4.question: Why is ice on the wings dangerous?
options: It changes the wing's shape and destroys lift
It makes the aircraft too shiny

q5.question: Why is landing with a tailwind dangerous?

q6.question: Why does a visual pilot never fly into a cloud?
options: Inside it you quickly lose all sense of up and down

q7.question: How does a headwind affect the take-off run?

q8.question: How does a pilot correct for a crosswind on landing?

q9.question: What should a pilot do if the forecast looks doubtful?
options: Fly higher to stay above the weather

q10.question: What does icing do to a wing?
options: It changes its shape and destroys lift
It makes it heavier but otherwise harmless

radio-alphabet.title: Radio and the spelling alphabet
radio-alphabet.description: Alfa, Bravo, Charlie — how pilots talk so nobody misunderstands.

why-standard.title: Why pilots talk 'funny'
why-standard.body: On a busy frequency, a misheard word can put two aircraft in the same place. That is why aviation uses standard phraseology: short fixed phrases with exactly one meaning each. It is not jargon for style — it is a safety tool shared by pilots around the world.

icao-alphabet.title: The spelling alphabet
icao-alphabet.body: Letters like B, D and E sound alike over a noisy radio, so each letter has a word: Alfa, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliett, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu. The registration D-EABC is read 'Delta – Echo Alfa Bravo Charlie'.

numbers.title: Numbers on the radio
numbers.body: Numbers are read digit by digit: runway 27 is 'two seven', not 'twenty-seven'. Nine is pronounced 'niner' so it cannot be confused with similar-sounding words. Headings, altitudes and frequencies are all spoken this way to leave no room for doubt.

q1.question: What is the spelling-alphabet word for the letter B?

q2.question: How do you read runway 27 on the radio?

q3.question: Why is 9 pronounced 'niner'?
options: To avoid confusion with similar-sounding words
Tradition from the age of sail

q5.question: What is the spelling-alphabet word for the letter A?

q6.question: What is the spelling-alphabet word for the letter S?

q7.question: What is the spelling-alphabet word for the letter Z?

q8.question: How do you read heading 270 on the radio?

q10.question: Why are numbers read digit by digit on the radio?
options: It is a legacy from Morse code

q11.question: What is the spelling-alphabet word for the letter C?

navigation-basics.title: Basic navigation
navigation-basics.description: Charts, courses and finding your way — from A to B without roads.

pilotage.title: Pilotage
pilotage.body: Pilotage means flying by looking outside and comparing what you see — rivers, roads, railway lines and towns — with the aeronautical chart. These large, linear features are easy to spot from the air and let you confirm your position at any moment. The golden rule is simple: you must ALWAYS know where you are on the chart.

dead-reckoning.title: Dead reckoning
dead-reckoning.body: Dead reckoning combines heading, airspeed and time to calculate where you will be at any given moment. Wind pushes you off your intended track, so pilots correct their heading by pointing 'into the wind' to compensate for that drift. A compass and a clock are the basic tools of dead reckoning — without them there is no calculation to make.

planning.title: Flight planning
planning.body: Before flying, pilots study the route, the expected weather, the fuel required plus a reserve, and choose an alternate aerodrome in case the destination is not usable. If you get lost in flight, climb higher: you will see more terrain and get better radio reception. Getting lost is nothing to be ashamed of — but hiding it instead of asking for help is dangerous.

q1.question: What does pilotage consist of?
options: Comparing terrain references with the chart
Always flying in a straight line without checking the map

q2.question: What is the golden rule of pilotage?
options: Always know where you are on the chart
Always fly at the maximum possible altitude
Never check the chart until the end of the flight

q3.question: Which three inputs does dead reckoning combine?

q4.question: What does the wind do to your intended track?
options: It pushes you off track; you correct by heading into the wind
It has no effect on the track at all
It only affects your speed, never your heading

q5.question: What are the basic tools of dead reckoning?
options: An altimeter and an airspeed indicator

q6.question: What is planned before a flight?
options: The route, the weather, the fuel and an alternate
Only the colour of the aircraft

q7.question: Why do pilots carry reserve fuel?
options: To cover the unexpected or a diversion
To make the aircraft fly faster
To save money at the destination airport

q8.question: What is an alternate aerodrome?
options: An option to land if you cannot land at the destination
The aerodrome where the aircraft gets repaired
A runway used only for night flights

q9.question: What should you do if you get lost in flight?
options: Climb higher and ask for help by radio
Turn off the radio to concentrate better

q10.question: What makes for good visual references from the air?
options: The colour of the sky at sunset
